messages.content:
```{r}
lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean=mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  left_join(., lemurs_df1, by="common_name") %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) + 
  geom_point(aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=sex), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="white") +
  scale_x_continuous(
  name = "Age at death (years)",
  breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
  labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Life expectancy by species") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

This code is used to create a plot showing the life expectancy of lemurs of different species. 

First, the code filters the lemurs_df dataframe to only include rows where the age_at_death_y column is not NA.

Then, the code calculates the mean age at death for each species using group_by and summarise functions. 

Next, the code joins the mean age at death values with the original dataframe using the common_name column as the key.

After that, the code rounds the mean values to one decimal place and creates a new column called "label" to store the rounded mean values as labels for the plot.

Then, the code uses the ggplot function to create a scatter plot, with the y-axis representing the species names in descending order of mean age at death and the x-axis representing the mean age at death values.

The code adds points to the plot to represent each individual lemur's age at death, with different colors indicating their sex. 

The code also adds a larger point with a different color to represent the mean age at death for each species.

Furthermore, the code adds text labels to each point representing the mean age at death.

Finally, the code customizes the x-axis labels and title, and the plot is displayed.

To add all `age_at_death_y` values to the scatter plot, you can simply remove the `geom_point()` line that filters the data based on sex. Here's the modified code:

```{r}
lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean=mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  left_join(., lemurs_df1, by="common_name") %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) + 
  geom_point(aes(x=age_at_death_y)) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  geom_text(aes(label=mean), size=2, color="white") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Age at death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Life expectancy by species") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

This modified code will now display all `age_at_death_y` values for each lemur, instead of filtering them based on sex.

```{r}
lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1_summary <- lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_age = mean(age_at_death_y))


lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  left_join(lemurs_df1_summary, by = "common_name") %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean_age))), x = mean_age, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(color = sex), shape = 21, size=6) +
  geom_point(aes(x=age_at_death_y)) +
  geom_point(size=6) +
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="Black") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Age at death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Life expectancy by species and sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

```

The modified code includes the `lemurs_df1_summary` dataframe to calculate the mean age at death for each species and sex combination. This allows for a separate color coding of points based on the sex of the lemurs.

Here's a breakdown of the modifications made to the code:

1. The `lemurs_df1_summary` dataframe is created by grouping `lemurs_df1` by `common_name` and `sex` and calculating the mean age at death using `summarise`.

2. In the `ggplot` function, the `geom_point` line that uses `lemurs_df1_summary` is added to plot the individual points representing each lemurs' age at death, with separate colors based on sex.

3. The `geom_point` line without specifying data is kept to add the larger points indicating the mean age at death for each species.

4. The color in `geom_text` is changed to "Black" for better visibility against the different point colors.

With these modifications, the scatter plot now shows the life expectancy by species and sex, with all `age_at_death_y` values included and color-coded by sex.

Apologies for the oversight. It seems that the variable `age_at_death_y` is not available in the provided code. I apologize for the confusion caused. 

If you confirm the name of the variable containing the age at death values, I will be able to provide you with an updated solution.
how to plot values from different data fraes

To plot values from different data frames, you can use the `geom_point` function multiple times, specifying the different data frames for each layer. Here's an example:

```{r}
# Assuming lemurs_df1 is one dataframe and lemurs_df2 is another dataframe
lemurs_df1_summary <- lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_age = mean(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df2_summary <- lemurs_df2 %>%
  group_by(common_name, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_age = mean(age_at_death_y))

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Comparison of Mean Age at Death by Species and Sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

In this example, I assume you have two data frames, `lemurs_df1` and `lemurs_df2`, each containing summary information about the mean age at death for different species and sex combinations.

The code first calculates the summary statistics for each data frame, creating `lemurs_df1_summary` and `lemurs_df2_summary`.

Then, inside the `ggplot` function, `geom_point` is used twice: one for `lemurs_df1_summary` and one for `lemurs_df2_summary`. Different aesthetics (`x`, `y`, and `color`) are specified for each layer to represent the different data frames.

The remaining code sets the x-axis labels, y-axis labels, plot title, and formatting options for the plot.

By using `geom_point` multiple times with different data frames, you can plot values from different data frames on the same plot.

Error in geom_text(aes(label = mean), size = 2, color = "Black") : ℹ Error occurred in the 2nd layer. Caused by error in `compute_geom_1()`: ! `geom_text()` requires the following missing aesthetics: x and y

Apologies for the oversight again. The `aes` function needs to be updated in the `geom_text` line to specify `x` and `y` aesthetics. Here's the corrected code:

```{r}
# Assuming lemurs_df1 is one dataframe and lemurs_df2 is another dataframe
lemurs_df1_summary <- lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_age = mean(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df2_summary <- lemurs_df2 %>%
  group_by(common_name, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_age = mean(age_at_death_y))

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Comparison of Mean Age at Death by Species and Sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

In this corrected code, the `geom_text` lines for each data frame (`lemurs_df1_summary` and `lemurs_df2_summary`) now specify the `x`, `y`, and `label` aesthetics to display the mean age at death values.

Please replace `lemurs_df1` and `lemurs_df2` with your actual data frames containing the required columns (`common_name`, `sex`, and `mean_age`) for the code to run successfully.

To specify the colors for `geom_point` based on a specific variable, you can use the `scale_color_manual` function. Here's an example:

```{r}
# Assuming lemurs_df1 is the dataframe for one layer
# Assuming lemurs_df2 is the dataframe for the other layer

# Assigning colors for each sex category
sex_colors <- c("Male" = "#FF0000", "Female" = "#0000FF")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = sex_colors) +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Comparison of Mean Age at Death by Species and Sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

In this code, "Male" is assigned the color `"#FF0000"` (red), and "Female" is assigned the color `"#0000FF"` (blue) in the `sex_colors` vector. 

The `scale_color_manual` function is then used to map the `sex` variable to the specified colors.

You can modify the `sex_colors` vector to assign different colors based on your preference or the specific sex categories in your data.

Please replace `lemurs_df1` and `lemurs_df2` with your actual data frames containing the required columns (`common_name`, `sex`, and `mean_age`) for the code to run successfully.

To specify colors for each sex category in the `geom_point` layer, you can use the `scale_color_manual` function and specify the colors manually. Here's an example:

```{r}
# Assuming lemurs_df1 is the dataframe for one layer
# Assuming lemurs_df2 is the dataframe for the other layer

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Male" = "#FF0000", "Female" = "#0000FF"),
    name = "Sex",
    labels = c("Male", "Female")
  ) +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = common_name, label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Comparison of Mean Age at Death by Species and Sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

In this example, the `scale_color_manual` function is used to manually specify the colors for each sex category. `"Male"` is assigned the color `"#FF0000"` (red), and `"Female"` is assigned the color `"#0000FF"` (blue).

The `name` argument in `scale_color_manual` is used to set the title for the color legend, and the `labels` argument is used to specify the labels within the legend.

Please replace `lemurs_df1` and `lemurs_df2` with your actual data frames containing the required columns (`common_name`, `sex`, and `mean_age`) for the code to run successfully.

To order the species by `mean_age`, you can add the `reorder` function to the `aes` mapping for the `y` aesthetic. Here's an example of how to modify the code:

```{r}
# Assuming lemurs_df1 is the dataframe for one layer
# Assuming lemurs_df2 is the dataframe for the other layer

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = reorder(common_name, mean_age), color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = reorder(common_name, mean_age), color = sex), shape = 21, size = 6) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Male" = "#FF0000", "Female" = "#0000FF"),
    name = "Sex",
    labels = c("Male", "Female")
  ) +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df1_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = reorder(common_name, mean_age), label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  geom_text(data = lemurs_df2_summary, aes(x = mean_age, y = reorder(common_name, mean_age), label = mean_age), size = 2, color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death (years)",
    breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30, 40),
    labels = c("0", "10", "20", "30", "40")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Species") +
  ggtitle("Comparison of Mean Age at Death by Species and Sex") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

In this example, the `reorder` function is added to the `aes` mapping for the `y` aesthetic in both `geom_point` and `geom_text`. This function reorders the `common_name` factor variable based on the `mean_age` value, so the species will be ordered by mean age in the plot.
